instructions: Each card's front shows the wine and a study question; the back shows the answer. Add new cards by copying an entry inside 'cards'.

cards.question: What are the three grapes that make up this Cava, and what characteristic does each one add?
cards.answer: Xarel·lo (60%) adds backbone and earthiness; Macabeo (25%) adds floral lift; Parellada (15%) adds acidity and elegance.

cards.question: What certification did this producer earn in 2022, and how many other Cava producers hold it?
cards.answer: The Elaborador Integral designation — Cava's equivalent of Récoltant-Manipulant — held by only 14 producers total in the D.O.

cards.question: How long has the Esteve family farmed this land, and what generation are they on?
cards.answer: Since 1597 — now the 4th generation.

cards.question: How long is this wine aged on the lees, and what does that aging give the wine?
cards.answer: 18–24 months on the lees, giving it a creamy texture, fine mousse, and toasted brioche notes.

cards.wine: Champagne, 'Les 3 Terroirs' Blanc de Blancs Extra Brut — Michel Gonet, 2020
cards.question: What grape is this Champagne made from, and what three flavors come specifically from its extended lees aging?
cards.answer: 100% Chardonnay; extended lees aging gives it hazelnut, toasted almond, and brioche notes.

cards.wine: Champagne, 'Les 3 Terroirs' Blanc de Blancs Extra Brut — Michel Gonet, 2020
cards.question: What are the three vineyard sites in this blend, and what does each contribute?
cards.answer: Le Mesnil sur Oger (Grand Cru chalk, tension), Montgueux (cool, citrus-driven), and Vindey in the Sézannais (rounder, fleshier texture).

cards.wine: Champagne, 'Les 3 Terroirs' Blanc de Blancs Extra Brut — Michel Gonet, 2020
cards.question: How many generations has the Gonet family owned this house, and who runs it today?
cards.answer: 7 generations — founded in 1802, now run by Sophie Gonet.

cards.wine: Champagne, 'Les 3 Terroirs' Blanc de Blancs Extra Brut — Michel Gonet, 2020
cards.question: What is the dosage level of this wine, and what style does that make it?
cards.answer: 2 g/L — very low, keeping the wine precise and mineral (a Blanc de Blancs Extra Brut).

cards.question: What grape makes up this rosé Champagne, and what winemaking method gives it its pink color?
cards.answer: 100% Pinot Noir; the color comes from saignée maceration — 48–72 hours of skin contact before pressing.

cards.question: How many Grand Cru villages source the fruit for this wine, and name two of them.
cards.answer: 10 Grand Cru villages, including Bouzy and Ambonnay (also Louvois and Tours-sur-Marne).

cards.question: Who created this cuvée, in what year, and what category did it start?
cards.answer: Bernard de Nonancourt, in 1968; it started the modern rosé Champagne category.

cards.question: What sales distinction does this wine hold today?
cards.answer: It's the world's best-selling rosé Champagne.

cards.wine: Champagne, 'Ultra Brut' Brut Nature — Laurent-Perrier, NV
cards.question: What two grapes make up this Champagne, and roughly what percentage of each?
cards.answer: ~55% Chardonnay, 45% Pinot Noir.

cards.wine: Champagne, 'Ultra Brut' Brut Nature — Laurent-Perrier, NV
cards.question: How much dosage (added sugar) does this wine have, and what does that make its taste style?
cards.answer: Zero dosage — completely dry, showing pure grape and chalk character with no sweetness.

cards.wine: Champagne, 'Ultra Brut' Brut Nature — Laurent-Perrier, NV
cards.question: What year was this style introduced, and what older house tradition does it revive?
cards.answer: Introduced in 1981; it revives the 1889 'Grand Vin Sans Sucre' style, originally made for British clients.

cards.wine: Champagne, 'Ultra Brut' Brut Nature — Laurent-Perrier, NV
cards.question: How many crus make up this blend, and how long does it rest on the lees?
cards.answer: 15 Grand and Premier Cru crus; at least 6 years on the lees.

cards.wine: Champagne, 'Grand Siècle No. 26' Brut — Laurent-Perrier, NV (2012, 2008, 2007)
cards.question: What two grapes make up this wine, and which one is the majority?
cards.answer: 58% Chardonnay, 42% Pinot Noir — Chardonnay-dominant.

cards.wine: Champagne, 'Grand Siècle No. 26' Brut — Laurent-Perrier, NV (2012, 2008, 2007)
cards.question: What three vintages are blended into this release, and in what proportions?

cards.wine: Champagne, 'Grand Siècle No. 26' Brut — Laurent-Perrier, NV (2012, 2008, 2007)
cards.question: How many Grand Cru villages source the fruit for this wine?
cards.answer: Eight.

cards.wine: Champagne, 'Grand Siècle No. 26' Brut — Laurent-Perrier, NV (2012, 2008, 2007)
cards.question: What is this cuvée named after, and what distinctive bottle design does it use?
cards.answer: Named for Louis XIV's 'Grand Siècle' (Great Century); bottled in a replica 17th-century flask.

cards.question: What grape is this Muscadet made from, and what mineral flavor is it especially known for?
cards.answer: 100% Melon de Bourgogne; known for crushed oyster shell and wet stone minerality.

cards.question: How many generations has the Luneau family farmed this land, and since when?
cards.answer: 9 generations, since the late 1600s.

cards.question: What two certifications does this estate hold?
cards.answer: Certified organic (Ecocert) and certified biodynamic (Demeter & Biodyvin).

cards.question: How does La Grange differ in style from the estate's other village wine, Le Verger?
cards.answer: La Grange is rounder and fleshier; Le Verger is firmer and more linear.

cards.question: What grape is this Sancerre made from, and what makes its texture different from typical Sancerre?
cards.answer: 100% Sauvignon Blanc; it's noted as unusually dense and full-bodied for the appellation.

cards.question: What soil type are the vines grown on, and what famous village shares this same soil?
cards.answer: Kimmeridgian Marne (Terre Blanche) — the same bedrock as Chavignol.

cards.question: Where did the two sons of the family train, and at what estates?
cards.answer: Victorien trained at Domaine Rousseau; Benjamin trained at Romanée-Conti.

cards.question: What yield restriction does this cuvée follow?
cards.answer: Restricted to 45 hl/ha.

cards.question: What grape is this wine made from, and what three flavors define its nose?
cards.answer: 100% Albariño; salt spray, white peach, and lemon define the nose.

cards.question: What soil type is this specific bottling grown on, and what does it contrast with in the winemaker's range?
cards.answer: Granite bedrock — contrasts with his schist-based bottlings.

cards.question: What is the winemaker's nickname, and what other winery does he work for?
cards.answer: "Chicho" — he's also the winemaker at Bodegas Fulcro.

cards.question: How old are the vines, and what traditional trellis system are they grown on?
cards.answer: 50+ years old, on the traditional pergola system on granite posts.

cards.question: What grape is this wine made from, and what two textural notes stand out on the nose?
cards.answer: 100% Chenin Blanc; beeswax and fresh cream stand out on the nose.

cards.question: Why is this wine labeled "Vin de France" instead of Vouvray?
cards.answer: Because Chidaine's cellar is in neighboring Montlouis, and the Vouvray AOC has barred him from using the Vouvray name since 2014.

cards.question: How large is the domaine today, versus when Chidaine inherited it in 1989?
cards.answer: 45 hectares today, up from just 4 hectares in 1989.

cards.question: What recognition has Chidaine received from La Revue du Vin de France?
cards.answer: He holds the highest rating of any Loire Valley producer — one of only seven to hold it.

cards.question: What grape is this Chablis made from, and what does the name "45°" refer to?
cards.answer: 100% Chardonnay; "45°" refers to the 45-degree slope of the vineyard the grapes come from.

cards.question: What vessel is this wine aged in, and why does that matter for its style?
cards.answer: Stainless steel — not oak — for 18 months, preserving the site's pure mineral character without oxidative influence.

cards.question: What two famous estates did Clément Lavallée work at before launching his own label?
cards.answer: Château Margaux and Armand Heitz (also Domaine Chapoutier and Rutherglen, Australia).

cards.question: Who are Lavallée's notable winemaking neighbors in Courgis?
cards.answer: Thomas Pico of Pattes Loup and Domaine De Moor.

cards.question: What two grapes make up this wine, and in what proportion?
cards.answer: A 50/50 field blend of Hondarribi Beltza and Hondarribi Zuri.

cards.question: Is the fizz in this wine natural or added, and how is it created?
cards.answer: Natural — bottle-conditioned residual CO₂ from fermentation, never force-carbonated.

cards.question: What category did this specific wine pioneer, and in what decade?
cards.answer: It was the first rosé Txakoli ever produced, in the 2000s.

cards.question: How old are some of the red grape vines used in this wine?
cards.answer: Some Hondarribi Beltza vines date back to 1840.

cards.question: What four grapes make up this rosé, and which one is the majority?
cards.answer: 60% Grenache, 20% Syrah, 14% Mourvèdre, 6% Rolle (Vermentino) — Grenache-dominant.

cards.question: What elevation is this vineyard at, and what distinction does that give it in the southern Rhône?
cards.answer: 530–630 meters — the highest vineyard in the southern Rhône.

cards.question: What historic building once stood on this property, and who owned it?
cards.answer: A medieval priory once owned by the Knights Templar.

cards.question: What wine did Decanter compare this to?
cards.answer: Domaine Tempier Bandol.

cards.question: What grape is this wine made from, and what makes its style "more Burgundian than Spanish"?
cards.answer: 100% Garnacha; its light body, delicate tannins, and floral character make it feel more Burgundian than a typical bold Spanish red.

cards.question: What elevation are the old vines grown at, and on what soil type?
cards.answer: 600 meters, on sandy granitic soils.

cards.question: Whose vineyards does this project continue, and who runs it today?
cards.answer: Continues the vineyards of Daniel Landi of Comando G; run today by his childhood friend, Curro Barreño.

cards.question: How does this bottling compare to the estate's higher-end cuvées?
cards.answer: It's more accessible and drinkable than the higher-altitude Las Uvas de la Ira (850m) and the single-vineyard Cantos del Diablo.

cards.question: What grape is this wine made from, and what neighboring village's style does it lean toward?
cards.answer: 100% Pinot Noir; it leans toward the elegance of neighboring Vosne-Romanée rather than typical Nuits-Saint-Georges.

cards.question: How many hectares does Faiveley farm in total, and how many Grands Crus do they own?
cards.answer: 120 hectares, including 12 Grands Crus.

cards.question: What two previously separate parcel bottlings now make up this single cuvée?
cards.answer: Aux Lavières and Les Argillats.

cards.question: What two critic scores has this wine received?
cards.answer: 91 points from Wine Advocate and 92 points from Jasper Morris.

cards.question: What two grapes make up this wine, and roughly what percentage of each?
cards.answer: ~80% Grenache, ~20% Mourvèdre.

cards.question: What does the estate's name, "Ancien Domaine des Pontifes," historically reference?
cards.answer: The property's historic ties to the Avignon papacy.

cards.question: Where is the cellar located, and what unique feature gives it thermal stability?
cards.answer: Roquemaure, built into a stone cliff (L'Escatillon).

cards.question: Since what year has this family produced wine here, and what generation are they on?
cards.answer: Since 1895 — 5th generation.

cards.wine: Bordeaux — Vieux Château Champs de Mars, 2003
cards.question: What two grapes make up this wine, and roughly what percentage of each?
cards.answer: 80% Merlot, 20% Cabernet Franc.

cards.wine: Bordeaux — Vieux Château Champs de Mars, 2003
cards.question: What made 2003 a distinctive vintage across France, and how did Merlot respond on the right bank?
cards.answer: 2003 was the heat-wave vintage across France; Merlot handled the extreme heat well, producing concentrated, plush wines.

cards.wine: Bordeaux — Vieux Château Champs de Mars, 2003
cards.question: Who founded this estate, and what winemaking philosophy did he pioneer at a time when Bordeaux was moving the opposite direction?
cards.answer: Régis Moro, in the early 1980s; he farmed organically and used low-intervention cellar work.

cards.wine: Bordeaux — Vieux Château Champs de Mars, 2003
cards.question: What neighboring, well-known estate shares a philosophy and friendship with this one?
cards.answer: Château Le Puy — whose owners, the Amoreau family, eventually acquired Champs de Mars.

cards.question: What grape is this wine made from, and what is its local Ribera del Duero name?
cards.answer: 100% Tempranillo, locally called Tinto Fino.

cards.question: What elevation and subzone is this vineyard located in, within Ribera del Duero?
cards.answer: Mambrilla de Castrejón, in the Burgos subzone — the highest and coolest part of the region, at 800–850m.

cards.question: What adjustments did the winemakers make for the warm 2024 vintage, and why?
cards.answer: They picked earlier and used shorter macerations to preserve freshness and acidity.

cards.question: Who runs this estate today?
cards.answer: A father-daughter team — Juan Carlos Vizcarra and his daughter Celia.

cards.question: What grape is this wine made from, and how does it develop its deep amber color?
cards.answer: 100% Macabeu; the amber color and dried-fruit, caramel character come from 21 years of deliberate barrel oxidation.

cards.question: What French winemaking technique stops fermentation and preserves the wine's natural sweetness?
cards.answer: Mutage — fortifying the wine with neutral grape spirit partway through fermentation to kill the yeast.

cards.question: What makes Domaine de Rancy unusual among Roussillon producers?
cards.answer: They're one of only a handful of estates still making traditional Ambré-style Rivesaltes, deliberately aging the wine oxidatively for decades.

cards.question: How long was this specific wine aged before bottling?
cards.answer: 21 years in oak, bottled in 2021.

cards.question: What three grapes make up this Sauternes, and which one dominates?
cards.answer: ~90% Sémillon, ~7% Sauvignon Blanc, ~3% Muscadelle — Sémillon-dominant.

cards.question: What natural phenomenon gives Sauternes its concentrated sweetness?
cards.answer: Botrytis cinerea, or "noble rot," which shrivels the grapes and concentrates their sugar.

cards.question: Who owns Château Rieussec today, and how prestigious is the estate within Sauternes?
cards.answer: Domaines Baron de Rothschild (Lafite), since 1984; it's one of the largest and most prestigious Sauternes estates, second only to Château d'Yquem.

cards.question: How is the fruit harvested to capture the right level of botrytis?
cards.answer: Pickers make several passes (tries) through the vineyard over 6–8 weeks, hand-selecting only individually shriveled berries.

cards.question: What two grapes make up this wine, and roughly what percentage of each?
cards.answer: ~80% Sémillon, ~20% Sauvignon Blanc.

cards.question: How much wine does a single Yquem vine typically produce?
cards.answer: About one glass.

cards.question: What company acquired Château d'Yquem, and in what year — the same year as this vintage?
cards.answer: LVMH, in 1999.

cards.question: What happens in vintages where botrytis and ripeness don't align?
cards.answer: No Grand Vin is made at all — the crop is sold off anonymously to other producers.

cards.question: What grape is this wine primarily made from?
cards.answer: Ugni Blanc.

cards.question: What makes a Pineau des Charentes different from a fermented wine like Sauternes or Rivesaltes?
cards.answer: It's a mistelle — fresh grape must is blended with Cognac before fermentation ever starts, rather than stopped partway through.

cards.question: How long has the Lhéraud family farmed in the Charente region?
cards.answer: Since 1680.

cards.question: What does "Très Vieux" mean, and how does this bottling compare?
cards.answer: It denotes a legal minimum of 10 years aged; this 1962 bottling has rested more than six times that minimum.

cards.question: What three grapes typically make up this Port?
cards.answer: Touriga Nacional, Touriga Franca, and Tinta Roriz.

cards.question: What does the "30 Years" on the label actually mean?
cards.answer: It's the official average age of all the wines blended together — not a single vintage.

cards.question: What makes Kopke historically significant?
cards.answer: Founded in 1638, it's recognized as the oldest Port house in the world.

cards.question: How does Tawny Port's aging process differ from Vintage Port's?
cards.answer: Tawny ages for decades in oak casks with steady oxygen contact; Vintage Port ages mostly sealed in the bottle.

cards.question: What grapes typically make up this Vintage Port?
cards.answer: Touriga Nacional, Touriga Franca, Tinta Roriz, and Tinto Cão.

cards.question: What makes a Vintage Port different from a Tawny?
cards.answer: It's declared only in the best years and matures mostly in the bottle, not in wood.

cards.question: How was 1994 regarded as a Port vintage?
cards.answer: It was widely and enthusiastically "declared" — Wine Spectator called it one of the best of the 20th century.

cards.question: What house produces this wine, and what's distinctive about its style?
cards.answer: Warre's, the oldest British Port house (founded 1670), known for a drier, more structured style.

cards.wine: Boston Bual — The Rare Wine Co.
cards.question: What two grapes make up this Madeira, and in what proportion?
cards.answer: 85% Bual, 15% very old Tinta Negra Mole.

cards.wine: Boston Bual — The Rare Wine Co.
cards.question: What aging method gives this wine its complexity, and how does it differ from cheaper Madeira production?
cards.answer: The canteiro method — barrels rest in the naturally warm upper floors of the wine lodge over years, rather than being quickly heated in tanks (estufagem).

cards.wine: Boston Bual — The Rare Wine Co.
cards.question: Why is this wine named "Boston"?
cards.answer: It's part of a series honoring American port cities where Madeira was historically popular — Boston favored sweeter styles like Bual.

cards.wine: Boston Bual — The Rare Wine Co.
cards.question: Why is old Tinta Negra Mole blended into an otherwise Bual wine?
cards.answer: To lend the depth and complexity of a true vintage Madeira to an otherwise non-vintage bottling.

cards.question: What grape is this wine made from, and what makes it the driest Madeira style?
cards.answer: 100% Sercial — grown at high elevation for maximum acidity, producing Madeira's driest, most age-worthy wines.

cards.question: What Portuguese term describes this wine's natural, decades-long aging process?
cards.answer: Canteiro — barrels are moved through the lodge to experience natural seasonal warmth and cooling.

cards.question: What does it mean for a Madeira to be classified as a "Frasqueira"?
cards.answer: A single-vintage, single-grape wine legally required to age at least 20 years before release.

cards.question: What is unusual about Barbeito's approach to color in their wines?
cards.answer: They never add caramel coloring — the deep amber-gold color here comes entirely from over a century of real aging.

cards.question: What grape is this wine made from?
cards.answer: 100% Bual (Boal).

cards.question: What is a "solera," and how does it apply to this wine?
cards.answer: A fractional blending system where wine is periodically drawn off and replaced with younger wine — so a trace of the founding 1845 vintage still runs through every bottle.

cards.question: Why was this solera originally created in 1875?
cards.answer: To help cope with the wine shortage caused by the Phylloxera epidemic.

cards.question: What distinction does Cossart Gordon hold in the Madeira trade?
cards.answer: Founded in 1745, it's the oldest company in the Madeira trade.

cards.question: What grape is this wine made from, and what style does it represent?
cards.answer: 100% Malvasia (Malmsey) — the richest and sweetest of Madeira's classic grapes.

cards.question: How does this wine differ from the Cossart Gordon Solera in terms of what's actually in the bottle?
cards.answer: This is a true Frasqueira — entirely 1834 fruit, with nothing blended in or refreshed, unlike a solera.

cards.question: Roughly how old is this wine?
cards.answer: Nearly two centuries old, from 1834.

cards.question: What family still runs the estate that produced this wine?
cards.answer: The de Vasconcelos family, who founded Vinhos Barbeito.
